g.data-narration: Welcome to Markov Chains - a powerful mathematical model for predicting random processes that evolve over time.
A Markov Chain is a random process where the future state depends ONLY on the present state, not on how we got here. This is called memorylessness.
g.text: A stochastic process where: P(Next State | Current State, History) = P(Next State | Current State)
The system forgets how it arrived at the current state. Named after Russian mathematician Andrey Markov (1856-1922).

g.data-narration: Markov Chains are used everywhere: Google PageRank, weather forecasting, speech recognition, and even modeling DNA sequences.
g.text: Why Learn Markov Chains? Real-World Applications

g.data-narration: A Markov Chain has states - possible conditions of the system. The set of all states is called the State Space S.
g.text: State (s i): A possible condition of the system (e.g., Sunny, Rainy, Cloudy)
State Space (S): The set of all possible states S = {s1, s2, ..., sn}

g.data-narration: Transition probability p_ij is the chance of moving from state i to state j in one step. This is the core of Markov Chains.
g.text: The probability of moving from state i to state j in exactly one step:
Probability that next state is j, given current state is i

g.data-narration: The Transition Matrix P contains all probabilities. Key property: every row sums to 1, because from any state, you MUST go somewhere.
g.text: Square matrix where entry (i,j) = pij. Two key properties:
- 0 less than or equal to pij less than or equal to 1 (all probabilities valid)
- Sum of pij = 1 (each row MUST sum to 1)

g.data-narration: Lets work through a weather example. From Sunny: 80 percent chance Sunny tomorrow, 20 percent chance Rainy. From Rainy: 60 percent Sunny, 40 percent Rainy.
We represent the current state as a probability vector. If it is Rainy today, our vector is 0, 1 - 0 percent Sunny, 100 percent Rainy.
g.text: Question: If it is Rainy today, what is the probability it will be Sunny on Day 2?
Initial state: v0 = [0, 1] - 0 percent Sunny, 100 percent Rainy

g.data-narration: To find Day 1 weather, multiply the initial vector by the transition matrix: v1 = v0 multiplied by P equals 0.6, 0.4.
g.text: v1 = v0 x P = [0, 1] x P

g.data-narration: For Day 2, we need P squared. Matrix multiplication: each entry is sum of row i times column j.
g.text: Matrix multiplication: (P squared)ij = Sum of P ik times P kj

g.data-narration: Finally, v2 = v0 times P squared equals 0,1 times P squared equals 0.72, 0.28. There is a 72 percent chance of Sunny weather on Day 2!
g.text: v2 = v0 x P squared = [0, 1] x P squared

g.data-narration: States can be classified: Absorbing states (p_ii = 1, cannot leave), Recurrent (will return), or Transient (might never return).
In the long run, the system reaches a Steady-State where pi = pi times P. For our weather, it is 75 percent Sunny, 25 percent Rainy.
g.text: The long-term equilibrium: pi = pi x P, where Sum of pi i = 1
For our weather model: solving pi x P = pi

g.data-narration: Key concepts: Markov Property (memorylessness), State Space, Transition Matrix (rows sum to 1), n-step probability, and Steady-State.
g.text: v n = v0 x P n

g.data-narration: Markov Chains are powerful tools for modeling random processes with memoryless transitions. They are essential for many CS applications!
g.text: Markov Chains: Foundation of Probabilistic Modeling
Understanding transitions, matrix operations, and steady-states enables modeling of complex random systems
